roots.title: Introduction to Logistic Regression and Classification
roots.summary: Logistic regression is introduced as a classification model, serving as a simpler version of neural networks. The transition from linear regression to classification is discussed, emphasizing the derivation of the logistic regression model and its importance in classification tasks. The segment contrasts regression and classification, focusing on the shift from predicting continuous outcomes to addressing broader machine learning problems, including supervised and unsupervised learning. It covers predicting categorical data, particularly in spam detection, and explores clustering methods for unlabeled data. An overview of supervised versus unsupervised learning is provided, highlighting the straightforwardness of supervised learning and distinguishing it from reinforcement learning. The taxonomy of machine learning is outlined, detailing labeled and unlabeled data, and various learning types such as regression, classification, and clustering. The segment emphasizes classification within machine learning taxonomy, introducing concepts of labeled versus unlabeled data, and quantitative and categorical response types, using examples like predicting tips based on total bills to illustrate the use of quantitative features.

children.title: Introduction to Logistic Regression and Classification
children.summary: Logistic regression is introduced as a classification model, serving as a simpler version of neural networks. The segment discusses the transition from linear regression to classification, highlighting both mathematical concepts and coding exercises. The derivation of the logistic regression model is emphasized for its significance in classification tasks and its foundational role in neural networks. It contrasts regression and classification, focusing on the shift from predicting continuous outcomes in linear regression to addressing broader machine learning problems, including supervised and unsupervised learning.

children.title: Introduction to Logistic Regression and Classification
children.summary: Logistic regression is introduced as a classification model, compared to a simpler version of neural networks. The segment discusses the transition from linear regression to classification, covering both mathematical concepts and coding exercises. The derivation of the logistic regression model is emphasized, highlighting its significance for classification tasks and its foundational role in neural networks. The discussion contrasts regression and classification, focusing on the shift from predicting continuous outcomes in linear regression to addressing broader machine learning problems, including both supervised and unsupervised learning.

children.title: Introduction to Logistic Regression and Classification
children.summary: The segment introduces logistic regression as a classification model, likening it to a simpler version of neural networks. It transitions from linear regression to classification, incorporating both mathematical concepts and coding exercises. The logistic regression model is derived, emphasizing its importance for classification tasks and its foundational role in neural networks. The discussion contrasts regression and classification, highlighting the shift from predicting continuous outcomes in linear regression to addressing broader machine learning problems, including supervised and unsupervised learning.

children.title: Introduction to Logistic Regression and Classification
children.summary: The lecture introduces logistic regression, emphasizing its role as a classification model, akin to a simpler version of neural networks. The focus will shift from linear regression to classification, incorporating both mathematical concepts and coding exercises. The goal is to derive the logistic regression model, marking a transition in the curriculum from regression techniques to classification tasks.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: All right, good. So good morning, everyone. Excited to cover a new topic. I guess I was just saying to the class here. I missed last week, which was simple. Hopefully you guys enjoyed The data processing the other set of tools on the data ecosystem. Normally I get to present those lectures but this time Rano was in Korea so I missed out on that. This week we're going to be doing logistic regression. Which is kind of like baby neural networks so it's really exciting. We're going to bring it a whole new set of models. There'll be a little bit of math maybe not as much as we had with regression. I will do some more code so it should be kind of fun mix of math and code today. So we're moving away from linear regression. I guess they should say we're moving away from regression. Because we're going to keep doing regression or say. children.title: Introduction to Classification and Logistic Regression
children.summary: The segment introduces the classification setting and derives the logistic regression model, which is essential for classification tasks and serves as a foundational element in neural networks. It contrasts regression and classification, noting that previous work focused on linear regression with labeled data, where the goal was to predict a continuous quantitative outcome. The discussion emphasizes the transition from linear regression to classification, highlighting the broader landscape of machine learning problems, including supervised and unsupervised learning.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And today we're going to introduce the kind of classification setting and we're going to derive the logistic regression model, which is the standard model used for classification. As I said, these are also the basic building blocks and neural networks. So, to first start talking about regression versus classification. So, up into this point, we've been building a... linear regression models doing linear regression and this picture I used in when I first taught this class actually in the machine learning landscape in the space of modeling problems are sort of let's say four main categories of modeling problems that people are counter so far we spent a lot of time on regression And you think this is a... I have labeled data so in all the regression work we've done so far we've had a why value. There's something we're trying to predict, so we had a label. We call these supervised learning problems. These are learning problems where I know what I'm trying to predict in my training data I have labels. And the label trying to predict is quantitative, uh, continuous. children.title: Predicting Categorical Data and Machine Learning Taxonomy
children.summary: The focus is on predicting categorical data, particularly in spam detection, and explores clustering methods for unlabeled data. An overview of supervised versus unsupervised learning is provided, highlighting the straightforwardness of supervised learning and distinguishing it from reinforcement learning, which is not included in the course. The taxonomy of machine learning is outlined, detailing labeled and unlabeled data, along with various learning types such as regression, classification, and clustering. Additionally, the segment emphasizes classification within machine learning taxonomy, distinguishing between supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning, and introduces concepts of labeled versus unlabeled data, as well as quantitative and categorical response types. The discussion transitions from linear regression to various machine learning techniques, using examples like predicting tips based on total bills to illustrate the use of quantitative features in regression.

children.title: Predicting Categorical Data and Learning Types
children.summary: The focus shifts to predicting categorical data, particularly in spam detection, and will explore clustering methods for unlabeled data. An overview of supervised versus unsupervised learning is provided, emphasizing supervised learning's straightforwardness and differentiating it from reinforcement learning, which is not covered in the course. The taxonomy of machine learning is outlined, detailing labeled and unlabeled data, as well as various learning types including regression, classification, and clustering.

children.title: Transition to Predicting Categorical Data
children.summary: The focus shifts to predicting categorical data, particularly in spam detection. The course will soon transition from labeled to unlabeled data, exploring clustering methods to identify groupings within datasets without pre-existing labels, such as differentiating between spam and non-spam emails based on their features.

children.title: Transition to Predicting Categorical Data
children.summary: The focus shifts to predicting categorical data, specifically in the context of spam detection. In the upcoming weeks, the course will transition from working with labeled data to unlabeled data. This will involve exploring clustering methods, where the goal is to identify groupings within a dataset without pre-existing labels, such as distinguishing between spam and non-spam emails based on their features.

children.title: Overview of Supervised vs. Unsupervised Learning
children.summary: The segment introduces supervised and unsupervised learning in machine learning, focusing primarily on supervised learning due to its straightforward nature compared to reinforcement learning. It discusses the differences between learning from rewards and human learning experiences, noting that reinforcement learning techniques like AlphaGo are not covered in the course. Additionally, it outlines the taxonomy of machine learning, including labeled and unlabeled data, and various learning types such as regression, classification, and clustering.

children.title: Overview of Supervised and Unsupervised Learning
children.summary: The discussion introduces the concepts of supervised and unsupervised learning in machine learning. It emphasizes that the focus will be on supervised learning, which is more straightforward than reinforcement learning, where the learner receives feedback after actions. The speaker compares learning from rewards to human learning experiences and mentions that reinforcement learning techniques, such as AlphaGo, are outside the scope of the course. The segment also highlights the taxonomy of machine learning, including labeled and unlabeled data, and different learning types such as regression, classification, and clustering.

children.title: Machine Learning Taxonomy: Classification Overview
children.summary: This segment provides an overview of machine learning taxonomy, focusing on classification. It distinguishes between supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning, and introduces labeled versus unlabeled data, as well as quantitative and categorical response types. The discussion transitions from linear regression to various machine learning techniques, emphasizing the use of quantitative features in regression to predict unbounded quantitative outputs, illustrated with examples like predicting tips based on total bills.

children.title: Machine Learning Taxonomy: Classification Overview
children.summary: The segment provides an overview of machine learning taxonomy, focusing on classification within the broader context of machine learning. It distinguishes between supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning, and introduces the concepts of labeled and unlabeled data, as well as quantitative and categorical response types. The discussion transitions from linear regression to exploring various machine learning techniques, emphasizing the use of quantitative features in regression to predict unbounded quantitative outputs, with examples such as predicting tips based on total bills.

children.title: Overview of Machine Learning Taxonomy
children.summary: The focus is on classification within the broader context of machine learning. The segment highlights the distinction between various types of learning problems, including supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning. It also introduces the concepts of labeled and unlabeled data, along with different response types such as quantitative and categorical. The discussion indicates a transition from linear regression to exploring other machine learning techniques, specifically classification.

children.title: Introduction to Machine Learning Taxonomy
children.summary: The lecture discusses the transition from linear regression to a broader understanding of machine learning problems. It highlights the distinction between different types of learning: supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning. In regression, quantitative features are used to predict a quantitative output, which can be unbounded. Examples include predicting tips based on total bills and goal differences based on turnover percentages.

children.title: Introduction to Classification and Data Visualization Techniques
children.summary: Classification in machine learning predicts qualitative outcomes from quantitative features, differing from regression. Features must be numerical, with applications like predicting days from bills or game winners from turnover percentages. The week's focus is on modeling class probabilities with the sigmoid function for binary outcomes, covering binary, multiclass, and structured prediction tasks. Techniques for binary classification will be foundational. The segment also discusses enhancing data visualization for binary outcomes using strip plots with jitter to reduce overplotting, improving clarity in distinguishing outcomes. Challenges in modeling basketball game outcomes with least squares linear regression are highlighted, particularly regarding outliers and invalid probabilities, suggesting a thresholding approach to maintain valid outputs. Data binning is introduced as an alternative to linear regression, using Pandas' 'cut' function to segment data into bins for better regression analysis.

children.title: Introduction to Classification in Machine Learning
children.summary: Classification is defined as a learning problem that predicts qualitative outcomes from quantitative features, contrasting with regression. Features must be converted into numerical values, with examples including predicting the day of the week from a bill or determining game winners based on turnover percentages. The focus for the week is on modeling class probabilities using the sigmoid function and linear techniques to predict binary outcomes. Classification types include binary (e.g., spam detection), multiclass (e.g., image labeling), and structured prediction tasks (e.g., translation). Foundational techniques for binary classification will be covered.

children.title: Introduction to Classification in Machine Learning
children.summary: Classification is defined as a learning problem that predicts qualitative outcomes from quantitative features, contrasting with regression. Features must be converted into numerical values, with examples like predicting the day of the week from a bill or determining game winners based on turnover percentages. The focus for the week is on modeling class probabilities using the sigmoid function and linear techniques, aiming to predict binary outcomes. Classification types are categorized into binary (e.g., spam detection), multiclass (e.g., image labeling), and structured prediction tasks (e.g., translation). Foundational techniques for binary classification will be covered.

children.title: Introduction to Classification in Machine Learning
children.summary: Classification is a different learning problem compared to regression, where quantitative features are used to predict qualitative (categorical) outcomes. Features such as text or categories must be converted into numerical values, and while some features like 'day of the week' can be modeled as categories, others like 'model of car' are more complex. Examples include predicting the day of the week from a total bill or predicting the winner of a game based on turnover percentage. The model will calculate probabilities for each class, and if the probability exceeds 0.5, a win is predicted.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So, that was regression. Now we're going to do classification. This is a different learning problem. Again, we have quite a data features. So we have to convert our features to numbers, real numbers. We've already talked about how to take features like text or categories and make those into numbers. Ah. Qualitative or kind of work with variables. Things like... Day of the week. You could have made that a number, but we might choose to model that as a category. There are seven days a week. Model the car, that's much harder to think of as a number. Hard to think how you would regress on predicting a model of a car. Video: 1: So Far: Regression

children.title: Introduction to Classification Problems
children.summary: This segment introduces classification problems using a basketball game as an example to determine which team will win. It outlines the focus for the week on modeling the probability of classes using the sigmoid function and linear modeling techniques. The aim is to predict binary outcomes, such as whether a team wins or loses, based on quantitative features. Examples include predicting the day of the week from total bill and modeling car types from horsepower. The segment emphasizes using probabilities to make predictions, with a decision rule based on a threshold of 0.5.

children.title: Classification Types: Binary, Multiclass, and Structured Prediction
children.summary: The discussion covers various types of classification tasks. Binary classification involves predicting one of two classes, such as spam or not spam, or win or lose. Multiclass classification extends this to multiple classes, with examples including image labeling and predicting the next word in a sentence. Structured prediction tasks aim to predict multiple related outputs, such as in translation or systems like ChatGPT, where the goal is to generate a sequence of outputs based on input.

children.title: Binary Classification Techniques Overview
children.summary: The segment discusses the foundational techniques for binary classification, highlighting that the class will cover neural networks and a tutorial on chat GPT. The focus is on predicting a binary outcome (0 or 1), with an explanation of notation variations (using 0 and 1 versus +1 and -1). The modeling process is outlined, emphasizing that the same approach applies across different machine learning tasks. Key steps include choosing a model (linear for regression), selecting a loss function (squared or absolute loss), and fitting the model using methods like scikit-learn or gradient descent, which will remain consistent throughout the course.

children.title: Modeling Process: Regression and Classification
children.summary: The modeling process involves several key steps: choosing a model (e.g., Linear Regression), selecting a loss function (such as Squared Loss or Absolute Loss), fitting the model using techniques like regularization and gradient descent, and evaluating model performance using metrics like R-squared and residuals. The discussion highlights that traditional evaluation metrics may not apply in new modeling domains. Additionally, logistic regression is introduced as a model used for classification tasks, clarifying that 'regression' pertains to the model type rather than the task.

children.title: Logistic Regression as a Classification Model
children.summary: Logistic regression is a classification model, despite its name suggesting otherwise. The term 'regression' refers to the type of model that incorporates a linear component, similar to traditional regression, but it is used for classification tasks. This distinction is important for understanding how logistic regression operates.

The logistic regression model will be utilized for this classification task, with an emphasis on understanding that 'regression' refers to the model type rather than the task itself. The games dataset provides details such as team names, matchups, win/loss indicators, and goal differences, with winning teams classified as 'Class 1'.

children.title: Field Goal Percentages and Game Outcomes
children.summary: The segment discusses the concept of field goal percentages in basketball, explaining how these statistics reflect a team's success in scoring. It highlights the significance of comparing the field goal success rates between teams, using Cleveland and Boston as examples to illustrate how a higher percentage can indicate a likely winner. The segment also introduces a dataset that tracks win/loss results of basketball games, including fields such as team names, matchups, and goal differences, categorizing teams that win their games as 'Class 1'.

children.title: Basketball Games Dataset Overview
children.summary: The games dataset provides win/loss results for basketball teams, detailing team names, matchups, win status, goal differences, and game IDs. It includes examples such as the Boston Celtics and Golden State Warriors, with specific statistics like field goal success rates. Teams that win are categorized as 'Class 1'.

children.title: Introduction to Basketball Games Dataset
children.summary: The games dataset provides information on win/loss results for basketball teams, including features such as TEAM_NAME, MATCHUP, WON, GOAL_DIFF, and GAME_ID. A simple demonstration is presented, comparing teams like the Boston Celtics and Cleveland Cavaliers, with a focus on the goal difference and win classification. The segment also discusses potential problems with using least squares linear regression for classification tasks, highlighting issues such as outputs falling outside the expected range and the interpretability of certain output values.

basketball = pd. read_csv("data/nba.csv")
first_team = basketball.groupby("GAME_ID") . first ( )
second_team = basketball.groupby("GAME_ID") . last ( )
games = first_team.merge(second_team, left_index = True, right_index = True, suffixes = ["", "_OPP"] )
games [ 'GOAL_DIFF' ] = games ["FG_PCT"] - games ["FG_PCT_OPP" ]
games ['WON' ] = (games ['WL' ] == "W") . astype(int)
games = games [ [ 'TEAM_NAME', 'TEAM_NAME_OPP', 'MATCHUP', 'WON', 'WL', 'GOAL_DIFF' ] ]

In this lecture, we will work with the basketball dataset, which contains information about basketball games played in the NBA. In the cell beld
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
cleaning to transform the data into a useful form, which we store as the DataFrame games .
Our goal in this portion of the lecture is to predict whether or not a team wins their game ( "WON" ) given their "GOAL_DIFF" . The variable "GOAL_DIFF"
represents the difference in successful field goal rates between the two teams competing in a game. A positive value for "GOAL_DIFF" means that a team made
more field goals than their opponent; a negative value means that the opponent made more field goals.

represents the difference in successful field goal rates between the two teams competing in a game. A positive value for "GOAL_DIFF" means that a team made

children.title: Data Processing for NBA Game Analysis
children.summary: The segment discusses using Python to analyze NBA game data. It demonstrates how to read a CSV file containing game statistics, group the data by game ID to separate the first and second teams, and merge these datasets to calculate goal differences and win/loss records. The resulting DataFrame includes team names, matchups, win indicators, and goal differences, providing a structured view of game outcomes.

If we visualize our data, we see a very different result from the scatter plots we have worked with for linear regression. Because a team can only win or lose a game,
the only possible values of "WON" are 1 (if the team won the game) or 0 (if the team lost).

Because the only possible values of "WON" are 0 or 1, the visualization above shows significant overplotting. We can use strip plot to jitter the y data by adding a
small amount of random noise. This makes the plot easier to interpret and gives us nice colors for 0 and 1.

games [ 'GOAL_DIFF' ] = games ["FG_PCT"] - games ["FG_PCT_OPP"]
games ['WON ' ] = (games ['WL' ] == "W") . astype(int)
games = games [ [ 'TEAM_NAME' , 'TEAM_NAME_OPP', 'MATCHUP', 'WON', 'WL', 'GOAL_DIFF']]
games

children.title: Enhancing Data Visualization and Regression Modeling
children.summary: The segment discusses improving data visualization for binary outcomes using strip plots with jitter to address overplotting issues in scatter plots. It emphasizes how jittering enhances clarity in differentiating between winning and losing outcomes. Additionally, it addresses challenges in modeling basketball game outcomes with regression, particularly the limitations of least squares linear regression due to extreme outliers and invalid probability values. A thresholding approach is suggested to keep outputs within the valid range. The segment also introduces data binning as an alternative to linear regression, using Pandas' 'cut' function to segment data into bins and compute average values, facilitating better regression analysis.

children.title: Improving Data Visualization with Strip Plots and Jitter
children.summary: The segment discusses the limitations of scatter plots for visualizing binary outcomes, particularly in game results where 'WON' is represented as 0 or 1. It highlights overplotting issues and introduces strip plots with jitter to enhance clarity by adding random noise, facilitating better differentiation between winning and losing outcomes. Jittering improves categorical data visualization, making the distribution and outliers more interpretable. Additionally, it covers fitting regression models to binary labels treated as continuous values, noting that least squares linear regression struggles with non-continuous y-values, and suggests using jittered labels to create clearer scatter plots.

children.title: Improving Data Visualization with Strip Plots
children.summary: The lecture discusses the limitations of scatter plots when visualizing binary outcomes, specifically in the context of game results where 'WON' can only be 0 or 1. It highlights the issue of overplotting in such visualizations and introduces the use of strip plots to add jitter to the y data, making it easier to interpret. This technique enhances the clarity of the visualization by introducing random noise, allowing for better differentiation between winning and losing outcomes.

children.title: Using Jitter in Strip Plots for Categorical Data
children.summary: Jittering is a technique used to improve the visualization of categorical data by adding random noise to the data points. In this case, a strip plot is employed to display the outcomes of games (win or lose) against goal differences. The visualization shows significant overplotting due to the binary nature of the 'WON' variable, which takes only values 0 (loss) or 1 (win). By applying jitter, the data becomes clearer and easier to interpret, allowing for a better understanding of the distribution and presence of outliers.

children.title: Fitting Regression Models with Jittered Labels
children.summary: A regression model is fitted to a dataset with binary labels (0 and 1) treated as continuous values. The least squares linear regression, which is typically used for continuous predictions, performs poorly due to the non-continuous nature of the y-values. To improve visualization, the labels are jittered by adding a small random uniform value, allowing for a scatter plot to be created with 'GOAL_DIFF' on the x-axis and the jittered 'WON' values on the y-axis.

children.title: Modeling Basketball Outcomes and Regression Challenges
children.summary: The discussion covers modeling basketball game outcomes using regression, specifically visualizing goal difference against win probability through scatter plots. It highlights the limitations of least squares linear regression, particularly regarding extreme outliers from teams like the Atlanta Hawks, which affect analysis. The segment also addresses the challenges of using linear regression for modeling probabilities, noting that negative values and values exceeding one are invalid. A thresholding approach is suggested to keep outputs within the 0 to 1 range, reinforcing the need for a new modeling method.

children.title: Modeling Basketball Outcomes with Regression
children.summary: The discussion focuses on visualizing basketball game outcomes using a scatter plot that correlates goal difference with win probability. It highlights the limitations of least squares linear regression for this data, suggesting the need for a new modeling approach. Extreme outliers and their impact on the analysis are noted, particularly referencing teams like the Atlanta Hawks and others, while emphasizing the inadequacy of traditional methods for certain teams.

children.title: Modeling Challenges with Linear Regression
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the limitations of linear regression, particularly the least squares method, in modeling probabilities. It highlights that negative numbers cannot represent probabilities, while positive numbers greater than one are also invalid. A suggestion is made to threshold the outputs to confine them within the range of 0 to 1. The need for a new modeling approach is emphasized, as the least squares linear regression is deemed ineffective for the current data set.

We could threshold it, and that would give us something that like stops at one, stops at zero. We want to try to build a better model. children.title: Graphing Averages and Binning for Regression Analysis
children.summary: The segment discusses an alternative approach to linear regression using data binning, where data is segmented into bins to compute average values of y for nearby x values, resulting in a line that approximates the regression line. The use of Pandas' 'cut' function for effective binning is introduced. Specifically, data is binned into 20 equally sized intervals for 'GOAL_DIFF' values, allowing for the computation of average 'JitterWON' values within each bin. The process of calculating win rates by bin center is explained, where average win rates are computed for each bin, leading to a visual representation of winning probabilities. Finally, a model is built to capture win rate behavior based on goal difference, highlighting a non-linear relationship between 0 and 1.

children.title: Graphing Averages for Regression Analysis
children.summary: The discussion focuses on an alternative approach to linear regression by utilizing the concept of averaging data within defined bins. Instead of fitting a regression line directly, data is segmented into bins, and the average value of y is computed for nearby x values. This method results in a line that approximates the regression line, demonstrating how to visualize relationships in the data. The use of Pandas' 'cut' function is introduced as a tool for binning the data effectively.

into bins of similar values, then computing the average value of y for each bin. This gives us a rough indication of the relationship between x and y.

children.title: Binning Data for Linear Regression Analysis
children.summary: The process of binning data into 20 equally sized intervals is discussed, specifically for the 'GOAL_DIFF' values. This method allows for the computation of average 'JitterWON' values within each bin, providing insights into the relationship between 'GOAL_DIFF' and 'JitterWON'. A brief overview of how to implement this using pandas is provided, including code snippets for creating bins and visualizing the data.

children.title: Calculating Win Rates by Bin Center
children.summary: The process involves computing an average for data points within specified bins, each associated with a center. By grouping all data that shares the same bin center, win rates can be calculated for each bin. This results in a visual representation of the probability of winning, showing a transition where certain conditions lead to consistent wins or losses.

hover_data=['TEAM_NAME', 'TEAM_NAME_OPP' ], height=800)
fig. add_trace(go. Scatter(x=win_rates_by_bin. index, y=win_rates_by_bin ['Win Rate'],
mode="markers+lines", name="Win Rate by Bin") )
for cut in cuts:
fig. add_vline(x=cut, line_dash="dash", line_color="black")
fig.show( )

children.title: Modeling Win Rate by Goal Difference
children.summary: The focus is on building a model to capture the win rate behavior based on goal difference, which is expected to be a non-linear relationship between 0 and 1. The discussion includes examining how this model reflects the observed data proportions.

children.title: Modeling Probability and Non-Linear Relationships
children.summary: A model is developed to estimate the probability of a team winning based on goal difference, using the average of labels within bins to calculate the proportion of wins. This empirical estimate results in a non-linear 'S' shaped probability curve, which will be important for future discussions. The method for calculating averages involves counting wins and dividing by the total data points in each bin. Additionally, the segment addresses transforming Y to model non-linear relationships in data, specifically for determining the probability of a datapoint belonging to Class 1. The challenge of using linear modeling techniques on a non-linear probability curve is tackled by applying transformations to linearize the relationship, fitting a linear model to the transformed variables, and then reverting the transformations to reveal the underlying relationship.

children.title: Modeling Probability with Non-linear Curves
children.summary: A model is being developed to estimate the probability of a team winning based on goal difference. The approach involves calculating the average of labels within bins, specifically the proportion of wins (y=1) in each bin. This empirical estimate serves as a model for the probability of winning at each goal difference. The resulting probability curve is noted to be non-linear, exhibiting an 'S' shape, which is significant for future discussions. The calculation method for averages in bins is outlined, emphasizing the counting of wins and dividing by the total number of data points in the bin.

children.title: Modeling Non-Linear Relationships in Data
children.summary: The focus is on transforming Y to address non-linear relationships in data, specifically modeling the probability of a datapoint belonging to Class 1. The challenge arises as the probability curve is non-linear while only linear modeling techniques are known. To address this, the approach involves applying transformations to linearize the relationship, fitting a linear model to the transformed variables, and then transforming the variables back to uncover the underlying relationship.

children.title: Modeling Odds and Linearizing Relationships
children.summary: The discussion emphasizes the significance of modeling odds in decision-making concerning probability, particularly for predicting outcomes such as win or lose scenarios. Odds are defined as the ratio of the probability of winning to the probability of losing and can be graphically represented. To linearize the S-shaped probability curve, the logarithm of the odds is utilized, resulting in a straight line. This transformation enables the application of a linear combination of features to effectively model the relationship, aiding in the classification of data points into their most likely categories.

children.title: Modeling Odds and Linearizing Relationships
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the importance of modeling odds in decision-making related to probability, particularly in predicting outcomes like win or lose scenarios. The odds, defined as the ratio of the probability of winning to the probability of losing, can be represented graphically. To linearize the S-shaped probability curve observed, the logarithm of the odds is taken, resulting in a straight line. This transformation allows for the use of a linear combination of features to model the relationship effectively, facilitating the assignment of data points to their most likely classes.

children.title: Linear Combination and Logistic Function in Regression
children.summary: A linear relationship between transformed variables is established using a linear combination of features, represented by the equation z = x0 + β1x1 + ... + βpxp, where z denotes the log(odds) aimed at predicting the probability of Class 1. The log(odds) is defined as z = log(p/(1-p)). The segment then models probability using the logistic function, derived from the log-odds, by exponentiating both sides to solve for P. The logistic function, or sigmoid function, is introduced with the equation p = e^(Xθ) / (1 + e^(Xθ)), illustrating the relationship between probability p, predictor variables X, and coefficients θ. Further, the algebraic manipulation to solve for p in relation to the logistic function is discussed, emphasizing the transformation back to original variables and the significance of the logistic function in modeling probabilities.

children.title: Linear Combination of Features in Regression
children.summary: A linear relationship between transformed variables is established, represented as a linear combination of features. The equation z = x0 + β1x1 + ... + βpxp is introduced, where z denotes the log(odds) and is not the final prediction but serves to remind us of the goal to predict the probability of Class 1. The log(odds) is defined as z = log(p/(1-p)).

children.title: Modeling Probability with Logistic Function
children.summary: A linear model is established using log-odds to derive a probability model P. The process involves exponentiating both sides to eliminate the logarithm, followed by solving for P. The logistic function, also known as the sigmoid function, is introduced in this context.

children.title: Logistic Function and Transformation Steps
children.summary: The segment discusses the transformation of variables in logistic regression, specifically solving for p in relation to the log-odds. It introduces the logistic function or sigmoid function, detailing the equation p = e^(Xθ) / (1 + e^(Xθ)), which relates the probability p to the predictor variables X and coefficients θ. The segment emphasizes the steps to transform back to the original variables and the significance of the logistic function in modeling probabilities.

children.title: Transforming Variables and Logistic Function
children.summary: The segment discusses the algebraic manipulation involved in solving for the probability p in relation to the logistic function, also known as the sigmoid function. It outlines the transformation back to original variables, detailing the relationship between p and the odds, and emphasizes the equation p = (1-p)e^x / (1 + e^x). The significance of the logistic function in modeling probabilities is highlighted.

children.title: Logistic and Sigmoid Functions in Regression
children.summary: The segment covers the sigmoid function, also known as the logistic function, used in logistic regression for transforming variables and calculating probabilities using the formula p = 1 / (1 + e^(-x^T)). It emphasizes the connection between log-odds and probability, presenting the logistic regression model that predicts the probability P of y equaling 1 based on feature x. The model is derived from log-odds and allows for predictions across all x values. Key properties of the sigmoid function are discussed, including its mapping of real numbers to probabilities, symmetry, reflection, and its derivative, which is essential for gradient descent. Understanding these concepts is crucial for applying the logistic regression model effectively.

children.title: Logistic and Sigmoid Functions in Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses the sigmoid function, also known as the logistic function, which is used in logistic regression. It explains how to transform back to original variables and solve for probability (p) using the formula p = 1 / (1 + e^(-x^T)). The relationship between log-odds and probability is highlighted, along with the mathematical representation of the logistic function.

children.title: Logistic Regression Model and Its Equation
children.summary: The segment presents the logistic regression model, which predicts the probability P of y equaling 1 given a feature x. The model is derived from the log-odds, expressed as sigma Oh equals 1 divided by 1 plus e to the negative t. The equation reflects a linear relationship with features, allowing predictions across all x values, not just within specific bins. Additionally, the discussion includes fitting a logistic regression curve to data, illustrating how the model can mirror computed averages while offering broader predictive capabilities.

The relationship between x ( "GOAL_DIFF" ) and y ( "WON" ) shows clear non-linearity. To apply the linear modeling techniques we've explored previously, we will
need to linearize the relationship. Recall our previous approach to transforming variables:
1. Apply transformations to linearize the relationship between our variables of interest.
2. Fit a linear regression model to the transformed data.
3. "Un-transform" our data to recover the underlying relationship between the original variables.
First, we need to decide what transformations we will perform. The S-shaped curve above doesn't resemble our bulge diagram, so we must apply a new
transformation to linearize the relationship.
To determine what this transformation should be, we make a few observations:
· All predictions on our curve are between 0 and 1.
To compute the average for each bin, we calculated:
# Y=1 in bin
# datapoints in bin
= P(Y = 1|bin).
Together, these observations indicate that our graph of averages is actually modeling the probability of a data point having Y = 1! Rather than predicting any
continuous numeric output, we now want to predict the probability of a datapoint belonging to Class 1 (i.e ., the team winning the game).
To determine if it is "likely" that a data point belongs to Class 1, we can consider the odds:

[ ]:
from plotly. subplots import make_subplots

fig. add_trace (go. Scatter(x=win_rates_by_bin. index, y=win_rates_by_bin[ 'Win Rate'], mode="markers+lines"), row=1, col=1)

fig. update_layout ( showlegend=False)
We've found a linear relationship! It turns out that the logarithm of the odds shares a linear relationship with our "GOAL_DIFF" feature. This means that we can
express the log odds as a linear combination of the features:

The relationship between x ( "GOAL_DIFF" ) and y ( "WON" ) shows clear non-linearity. To apply the linear modeling techniques we've explored previously, we will
need to linearize the relationship. Recall our previous approach to transforming variables:
1. Apply transformations to linearize the relationship between our variables of interest.
2. Fit a linear regression model to the transformed data.
3. "Un-transform" our data to recover the underlying relationship between the original variables.
First, we need to decide what transformations we will perform. The S-shaped curve above doesn't resemble our bulge diagram, so we must apply a new
transformation to linearize the relationship.
To determine what this transformation should be, we make a few observations:
· All predictions on our curve are between 0 and 1.
To compute the average for each bin, we calculated:
# Y=1 in bin
# datapoints in bin
= P(Y = 1|bin).
Together, these observations indicate that our graph of averages is actually modeling the probability of a data point having Y = 1! Rather than predicting any
continuous numeric output, we now want to predict the probability of a datapoint belonging to Class 1 (i.e ., the team winning the game).
To determine if it is "likely" that a data point belongs to Class 1, we can consider the odds:

Together, these observations indicate that our graph of averages is actually modeling the probability of a data point having Y = 1! Rather than predicting any
continuous numeric output, we now want to predict the probability of a datapoint belonging to Class 1 (i.e ., the team winning the game).
To determine if it is "likely" that a data point belongs to Class 1, we can consider the odds:

children.title: Key Points on the Sigmoid Function
children.summary: The sigmoid function is crucial for upcoming assignments, as it maps any real number to a probability between 0 and 1, making it suitable for regression problems. The function is defined as o(t) = 1 / (1 + e^(-t)), with a domain of all real numbers and a range between 0 and 1. Important properties include its symmetry, reflection, and derivative, which is given by d/dt o(t) = o(t)(1 - o(t)). The inverse of the sigmoid function is t = log(p / (1 - p)). Understanding these aspects is essential for applying the logistic regression model.

children.title: Properties and Derivatives of the Sigmoid Function
children.summary: The sigmoid function, an S-shaped curve, transforms values into probabilities between 0 and 1, with important properties such as symmetry and its inverse relationship with 1 minus p. The derivative of the sigmoid function is useful for implementing gradient descent, which is relevant for courses like CS189. Key identities include the relationship between 1 minus p and the sigmoid function, as well as the derivative expressed in a simplified form.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: into a thing between zero and one a probability. This is a very standard way to take something that is not a probability and turn it into a probability. It has a few important properties. So if I take 1 minus, so 1 minus p can be written, this is 1 minus sigma of t, and we're sigma t is modeling p, so 1 minus p for example, can be written as sigma of negative t. The symmetry. It's inverse, what we derived is the law gods. That's why we built the thing in the first place, based on law gods. I don't think you need to know that for this class anymore, but we used to use the derivative of the sigmoid to actually implement gradient descent. So if you did want to implement gradient descent by hand, you would use this property that I can take the derivative of this function as this really nice simple form. children.title: Sigmoid Function and Logistic Regression Model
children.summary: The segment discusses the sigmoid function, represented by the equation Y = 1 / (1 + e^(-t)), which describes the S-shaped curve derived in previous discussions. It explains how adjusting the intercept affects the curve's shape. The segment also introduces the logistic regression model for predicting the probability of a data point belonging to Class 1, using an example of predicting a team's win probability based on features like goal difference and free throws, and mentions fitting a logistic regression model with estimated parameters.

children.title: Exploring Coefficients in Linear Models
children.summary: The discussion focuses on how adjusting the coefficients in a linear model affects its shape and behavior. As one coefficient increases, the model becomes steeper, while decreasing it makes the slope shallower until it flips to negative. The presenter emphasizes the importance of tuning these parameters to best fit the data, illustrating the concept with a graph and encouraging questions about the implications of these changes.

children.title: Logistic Regression, Sigmoid Function, and Exponential Behavior
children.summary: The logistic regression model predicts outcomes based on input features using the sigmoid function, which transforms numerical features into probabilities for binary outcomes. The segment clarifies that 'sigma' will denote the sigmoid function in future discussions, differentiating it from standard deviation. A graphical representation highlights the sigmoid function's S-shaped curve, domain, range, symmetry, and derivatives. The logistic regression model is detailed with the formula Po(Y = 1 | x) = 1 / (1 + e^(-x^Tθ)), explaining how log-odds is a linear combination of x and parameters. The sigmoid function approaches 1 asymptotically, illustrated through a graph. Additionally, the behavior of exponential functions with varying coefficients is examined, showing how coefficients influence the function's transition between values and the implications of including multiple features in the model.

children.title: Logistic Regression and Sigmoid Function
children.summary: The logistic regression model provides an estimated probability for predicting outcomes based on input features. The sigmoid function, denoted as sigma in this context, transforms numerical features into probabilities, allowing for predictions of binary outcomes. The segment also clarifies that sigma will refer to the sigmoid function in upcoming lectures, distinguishing it from its common meaning of standard deviation. A graphical representation of the sigmoid function is discussed, illustrating its S-shaped curve and properties such as domain, range, symmetry, and derivatives.

children.title: Logistic Regression Model and Sigmoid Function
children.summary: The segment discusses the logistic regression model, which models the probability P(Y = 1 | x) using a sigmoid transformation of a linear model. It explains that the log-odds is a linear combination of x and parameters, leading to the formula Po(Y = 1 | x) = 1 / (1 + e^(-x^Tθ)). The sigmoid function approaches 1 asymptotically but never actually reaches it, illustrated through a graph showing the behavior of the function as x varies.

children.title: Behavior of Exponential Functions with Coefficients
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the behavior of an exponential function as its coefficients change. When the expression inside the parentheses is negative, the overall exponent becomes positive, leading to the limit of one over one plus a large number approaching zero. This indicates that the signal's behavior can be controlled by the coefficients, affecting how quickly it transitions between values. Adjusting the coefficients can flip the curve direction, resulting in different behaviors for positive and negative values. The conversation also touches on the implications of having more than two features in the model.

children.title: Feature Representation and Logistic Regression Example
children.summary: The discussion covers the use of multiple features in modeling, such as in spam classification, highlighting that complex representations in higher dimensions can be visualized as curved surfaces. An example of logistic regression is presented, predicting the probability of a basketball team winning a game using two features: GOAL_DIFF and the number of free throws. The optimal parameters estimated from training data are 0.1 and -0.5, and the calculation demonstrates predicting the probability for a new team based on specific feature values.

children.title: Feature Representation in Higher Dimensions
children.summary: The discussion covers the concept of using multiple features in modeling, particularly in contexts like spam classification. It emphasizes that more than one feature can be included in the analysis, allowing for complex representations in higher dimensions, which can be visualized as curved surfaces.

The model uses two features: GOAL_DIFF and the number of free throws. The optimal parameters estimated from training data are given as 0.1 and -0.5. The calculation involves predicting the probability for a new team with specific values for these features.

children.title: Logistic Regression Probability Calculation Example
children.summary: A logistic regression model predicts the probability of a team winning a game based on features GOAL_DIFF and the number of free throws. The model is fitted without an intercept, with estimated parameters of 0.1 for GOAL_DIFF and -0.5 for free throws. An example calculation for a new team using these features demonstrates the application of the logistic function, resulting in a predicted probability of approximately 0.7311.

children.title: Logistic Regression Example Calculation
children.summary: A logistic regression model is used to predict the probability of a team winning a game based on two features: GOAL_DIFF and the number of free throws. The model is fitted without an intercept, and the estimated parameters are given as 0.1 for GOAL_DIFF and -0.5 for free throws. An example calculation is presented to predict the probability for a new team with specific feature values, demonstrating the use of the logistic function.

children.title: Logistic Regression Example Calculation
children.summary: The segment discusses predicting the probability of a team winning a game using logistic regression. It involves fitting a model without an intercept using training data, estimating optimal parameters as [0.1, -0.5], and calculating the probability for a new team with features GOAL_DIFF and number of free throws. The calculation results in a probability of approximately 0.7311, demonstrating the application of the logistic regression model.

children.title: Linear Models, Sigmoid Functions, and Logistic Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses the mapping of values in a linear model, emphasizing the intercept term set to zero and the coefficient set to one, along with visualizing the model using scatter plots in Python to illustrate win rates. It explains the sigmoid function, detailing how shifts affect its zero point, with an output of 0.5 when zero is input. The behavior of a function with zero coefficients is analyzed, showing that the output stabilizes at 0.5. A basic probability model is introduced with an initial winning chance of 0.5, explaining how varying parameters shift the transition point and influence winning probabilities. Lastly, the properties of a logistic regression model are examined, highlighting the role of theta_0 in curve positioning and theta_1 in steepness.

children.title: Linear Model and Graph Visualization
children.summary: The segment discusses the mapping of values in a linear model, specifically mentioning the intercept term set to zero and the coefficient set to one. It also covers visualizing the model using scatter plots in Python, illustrating win rates by bin and adding vertical lines to the graph for cuts.

children.title: Understanding Sigmoid Function Transformations
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the sigmoid function, specifically how shifting the function left or right affects its zero point. When zero is plugged into the function, the output is 0.5. Additionally, scaling the function alters the rate of transition between its values, impacting how quickly it moves from one side to the other.

leaf_chunk.text:

Just shifting where zero occurs, right? So here zero is zero. What happens when I plug in zero here? Let's eat in zero. What's easy to do? One. 1 over 1 plus one two one half. So, add 0, it's 0.5. But I can scale it. I can let's scale it. So if I change this this thing right here, that's changing how quickly it transitions from one side to the other, right? Video: 1: Untitled Graph

children.title: Understanding Zero Coefficients in Functions
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the behavior of a function when both coefficients are set to zero. By substituting these values, it is shown that the output becomes consistently 0.5, as the exponential term simplifies to e^0, which equals 1. This leads to the conclusion that the function stabilizes at 0.5 under these conditions.

children.title: Modeling Probabilities and Transition Curves
children.summary: The discussion begins with a basic model of probability, where the initial chance of winning is set at 0.5, serving as a neutral starting point. The lecturer explains how varying parameters can shift the transition point from zero to one, affecting the winning probabilities based on different values of x. The interaction between features is explored, indicating that adjustments to these parameters influence the curve's behavior. It is noted that there is no analytic solution to the problem presented.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Right. So my model if I set everything to zero is just what's the chance of them winning point five. Not a bad place to be, right? So I don't know anything with chance of winning. Bye-bye. We'll see later that maybe that's that we want. Maybe, you know, TMA always wins because home advantage or something. But yeah, this gives us our basic starting point. Oh, typically we have this thing not zero, so we have some transition from zero to one. And if I go past zero make it negative, I flip the way it transitions. So now smaller values of x. Win and larger values of x lose. Alright, let me go steep again this way. And maybe it's not at zero that this transition happens maybe it's over here at one. Over here. Okay, next you interact. So if I move this... If I make this smaller, it also transitions the curve shifts over as well. So do they interact between these two features? And how do we turn these features for these primators? children.title: Properties of the Logistic Model Explained
children.summary: The segment discusses the properties of a logistic regression model with one feature and an intercept term. It explains that the parameter theta_0 controls the position of the curve along the horizontal axis, while the magnitude of theta_1 influences the steepness of the sigmoid curve. Additionally, the sign of theta_1 determines the orientation of the curve, indicating how it flips. The segment concludes with an invitation for questions regarding the sigmoid function.

children.title: Properties of the Logistic Regression Model
children.summary: The logistic regression model with one feature and an intercept term is defined as p = P(Y=1 | x) = 1 / (1 + e^-(β0 + β1x)). Key properties include: β0, which controls the position of the curve along the horizontal axis; the magnitude of β1, which determines the steepness of the sigmoid curve; and the sign of β1, which affects the orientation of the curve.

children.summary: Discussion on food classification using neural networks highlights a humorous example from Silicon Valley, illustrating binary classification challenges with images like hot dogs and pizzas. The segment emphasizes the complexities of image classification in AI, where modern models aim to predict among 120,000 classes. Additionally, it covers fitting logistic regression models, explaining that they predict probabilities instead of direct classifications, with a focus on interpreting the probability matrix.

children.title: Food Classification with Neural Networks

children.title: Challenges in Image Classification and Research Focus
children.summary: Classification is a significant challenge in AI, particularly in dealing with confusing images. Research is heavily focused on developing better classifiers, with modern models attempting to predict one of 120,000 classes. The discussion emphasizes the complexity of classification problems and the foundational elements necessary for building effective classification models.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Um. Classification is hard. And like a lot of high research actually deal with these sorts of problems. So like how to deal with images that are very confusing. So we spend a lot of time thinking about building better classifiers. It's a big focus of research. And as I said earlier, the latest generation of models are also the gender of models are built using these same ideas, trying to predict one of 120,000 different classes at every step. All right. So classification really big, Part of AI is driving a lot of things and is a hard problem. children.title: Fitting Logistic Regression Models
children.summary: Logistic regression is similar to other regression models in terms of fitting. The model predicts probabilities rather than direct classifications. The speaker demonstrates fitting a logistic regression model and emphasizes that predictions yield probabilities for outcomes, specifically noting the importance of interpreting the second entry in the probability matrix.

children.title: Logistic Regression Model and Loss Functions Overview
children.summary: The segment covers the logistic regression model, emphasizing the logistic function represented as the sigmoid function and the importance of selecting an appropriate loss function for evaluation. It presents the model as y = P(Y = 1|x) = o(x'0) and includes a code example for fitting a logistic regression model using a software package, along with a visualization of predictions. The discussion on loss functions contrasts squared loss with cross-entropy loss, explaining why squared loss is unsuitable for logistic regression. A toy dataset is introduced to illustrate predicting a binary target. The computation of mean squared error (MSE) is detailed, including calculating squared errors and visualizing the loss surface, which is shown to be non-convex, indicating potential local and global minima. The segment concludes with a discussion on the characteristics of the MSE loss curve and the identification of its minimum point.

children.title: Logistic Regression Model and Loss Function
children.summary: The segment discusses fitting and predicting with a logistic regression classifier, highlighting the logistic function and its representation as the sigmoid function. It emphasizes the importance of choosing an appropriate loss function for evaluating logistic regression techniques. The model is expressed as y = P(Y = 1|x) = o(x'0), and the segment includes a code example demonstrating how to fit a logistic regression model using a software package. Visualization of the model's predictions is also mentioned.

children.title: Choosing Loss Functions in Logistic Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses the selection of loss functions for logistic regression, specifically considering the use of squared loss versus cross-entropy loss. It highlights that while squared loss is familiar, it is not suitable for logistic regression. A toy dataset is introduced to illustrate the process of using a feature to predict a binary target, setting the stage for further exploration of loss functions in model fitting.

children.title: Mean Squared Error and Loss Surface Exploration
children.summary: The segment discusses the computation of mean squared error (MSE) for predictions made using a sigmoid function. It explains how to calculate the squared error between predicted values and true labels, and then take the mean of those errors. A plot of the loss surface for toy classification data is introduced, showing that the loss surface is not convex, indicating the presence of both local and global minima. This characteristic implies that different starting points in optimization can lead to different results. The segment also includes code for visualizing the MSE on toy data and demonstrates the use of optimization techniques to find the best parameters.

children.title: Mean Square Error Loss and Convexity Discussion
children.summary: The segment discusses the mean square error (MSE) loss function related to a specific model's parameters, highlighting the characteristics of the loss curve. It identifies the minimum point on the curve and notes its non-convex nature, indicating challenges in optimization due to the presence of multiple minima. A practical example is provided, illustrating how starting at a certain point can lead to finding a local minimum. Additionally, the segment includes a code snippet for plotting the loss surface for toy data using MSE with a sigmoid function, demonstrating the relationship between model parameters and loss.

Let's plot the loss surface for this toy data using MSE with the model y = o(x. 0). We don't include an intercept term, so 0 and x are both scalars.
[24] :
def sigmoid (z) :
return 1/(1+np.exp(-z))
def mse_on_toy_data ( theta):
p_hat = sigmoid ( toy_df [ 'x' ] * theta)
return np.dean((toy_df ['y'] - p_hat ) ** 2)
theta_loss = pd. DataFrame({"theta": np. linspace (-10, 10, 100)})
theta_loss ["MSE"] = theta_loss ["theta"] . apply (mse_on_toy_data)
px. line ( theta_loss, x="theta", y="MSE", width=800,
title="MSE on Toy Classification Data")

children.title: Logistic Regression Curve Optimization
children.summary: A logistic regression curve was plotted, indicating that small x values correspond to y=0 and large x values to y=1, with uncertainty in the middle range. The loss surface for the model was discussed, highlighting its non-convexity, which results in multiple local and global minima. An optimization process using scipy's minimize function was demonstrated, starting with an initial guess of theta=0, which yielded an optimized value of approximately 0.54. A model was then visualized with this optimized theta, and the next steps involved trying different starting points for the optimization.

children.title: Optimizer Behavior and Loss Surface Analysis
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the optimizer's performance starting at an initial guess of theta = -5, which leads to a flat region indicating a local minimum on the loss surface. A plot of the mean squared error (MSE) is presented, showing that the loss surface is not convex, containing both local and global minima. The function mse_on_toy_data is defined to calculate the MSE based on the logistic regression model, demonstrating strange behavior when the optimizer is stuck at a local minimum.

children.title: Challenges with Non-Convex Loss Functions
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the non-convex nature of a specific loss function, which complicates optimization routines. Despite the loss function having a minimum that is not ideal, finding the true minimum is still considered beneficial. The segment includes a comparison of two data points derived from different optimizer setups, illustrating how they relate to local and global minima on the loss surface. A plot is referenced to visualize these optimized guesses against the original loss surface.

We've seen now that the fact that MSE is not convex for logistic regression leads to difficulty in optimizing .
Beyond this issue, the squared loss isn't well-suited for a probability task. Since p; is between 0 and 1, and y; is either 0 or 1, the squared loss for a single point
(yi - pi)2 is bounded between 0 and 1.
What this means in practice: Even if our prediction is terrible, the squared loss is never that large. Consider the "worst-case scenario" where the true class y; of
datapoint i is 0, and the model predicts a probability pi = 1 that this datapoint belongs to Class 1. Even though our model has made the worst possible prediction, the
squared loss is only (0 - 1)2 = 1!

Motivating Cross-Entropy Loss

We've seen now that the fact that MSE is not convex for logistic regression leads to difficulty in optimizing .
Beyond this issue, the squared loss isn't well-suited for a probability task. Since p; is between 0 and 1, and y; is either 0 or 1, the squared loss for a single point
(yi - pi)2 is bounded between 0 and 1.
What this means in practice: Even if our prediction is terrible, the squared loss is never that large. Consider the "worst-case scenario" where the true class y; of
datapoint i is 0, and the model predicts a probability pi = 1 that this datapoint belongs to Class 1. Even though our model has made the worst possible prediction, the
squared loss is only (0 - 1)2 = 1!

We've seen now that the fact that MSE is not convex for logistic regression leads to difficulty in optimizing .
Beyond this issue, the squared loss isn't well-suited for a probability task. Since p; is between 0 and 1, and y; is either 0 or 1, the squared loss for a single point
(yi - pi)2 is bounded between 0 and 1.
What this means in practice: Even if our prediction is terrible, the squared loss is never that large. Consider the "worst-case scenario" where the true class y; of
datapoint i is 0, and the model predicts a probability pi = 1 that this datapoint belongs to Class 1. Even though our model has made the worst possible prediction, the
squared loss is only (0 - 1)2 = 1!

children.title: Challenges of MSE in Logistic Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses the non-convexity of Mean Squared Error (MSE) in logistic regression, which complicates optimization. It highlights that the squared loss is not ideal for probability tasks since the predicted probability (p) is confined between 0 and 1, while the actual outcomes (y) are binary (0 or 1). The squared loss for a single prediction is limited to a range of 0 to 1, meaning that even in the worst-case scenario, where the prediction is entirely incorrect, the squared loss only reaches a maximum of 1.

children.title: Loss Functions and Decision Thresholds in Classification
children.summary: The segment explains squared loss and cross-entropy loss, detailing how p-hat values impact loss calculations, with minimal loss occurring when p-hat is near one and significant penalties at zero. Squared loss is defined as (0 - 1)² = 1, and L2 loss is visualized using a DataFrame. Negative log loss is introduced for true observations of 1, leading to an understanding of cross-entropy loss, which is preferred for classification due to its steep penalties for incorrect predictions and its convex nature, aiding optimization. Challenges of squared loss, including its non-convex nature and local minima issues, are discussed with examples. The segment also covers decision thresholds, commonly set at 0.5, and notes that this may not be optimal for cases with unequal probabilities, with future lectures planned to address tailored decision rules. It highlights the adaptability of the model for complex applications, explores decision boundaries and data separability in higher dimensions, and emphasizes the unpredictability of sports outcomes and the significance of performance metrics.

children.title: Loss Functions: Squared Loss and Cross-Entropy
children.summary: The segment explains squared loss and cross-entropy loss, highlighting how p-hat values affect loss calculations, with p-hat near one resulting in minimal loss and zero leading to significant penalties. It defines squared loss as (0 - 1)² = 1 and visualizes L2 loss using a DataFrame. Negative log loss is introduced, particularly for true observations of 1, leading to an understanding of cross-entropy loss. Challenges of squared loss are discussed, particularly its non-convex nature causing local minima issues in optimization, illustrated with mean squared error examples. Cross-entropy loss is favored for classification due to its steep penalties for incorrect predictions, while squared loss is criticized for its bounded nature. The segment also covers model selection, evaluation steps, and the definition of cross-entropy loss for binary labels, emphasizing its behavior at extreme probabilities. Finally, it notes the convex nature of cross-entropy loss as a key optimization function in software packages.

children.title: Squared Loss and Cross-Entropy Loss Explained
children.summary: The segment discusses the implications of p-hat values on loss calculations, where a p-hat close to one results in minimal loss, while a p-hat of zero incurs a significant penalty, necessitating a steeper penalty curve for incorrect predictions. It introduces squared loss, defined as (0 - 1)² = 1, and visualizes L2 loss as a function of p-hat values using a DataFrame. The concept of negative log loss, defined as l = -log p, is also introduced, particularly for true observations of 1, leading to an understanding of cross-entropy loss. Additionally, the challenges of squared loss are highlighted, focusing on its non-convex nature, which can lead to multiple minima in optimization and cause models to get stuck in local minima, as illustrated with mean squared error (MSE) examples on toy classification data. The effects of different initial guesses in gradient descent are discussed, showing their influence on achieving different optimal estimates.

children.title: Understanding Squared Loss and Cross-Entropy Loss
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the implications of p-hat values in relation to loss calculations. A p-hat value close to one results in minimal loss, while a p-hat of zero incurs a significant penalty, indicating the need for a steeper penalty curve for incorrect predictions. The segment introduces squared loss, defined as (0 - 1)² = 1, and presents a DataFrame to visualize L2 loss as a function of p-hat values. It then transitions to the concept of negative log loss, defined as l = -log p, particularly when the true observation is 1, setting the stage for understanding cross-entropy loss.

children.title: Challenges of Squared Loss and Non-Convexity
children.summary: The discussion highlights the non-convex nature of squared loss, emphasizing that a line connecting two points on the curve does not remain above the surface, leading to multiple minima during optimization. It points out that squared loss can cause models to get stuck in local minima, illustrated with examples of mean squared error (MSE) on toy classification data. The impact of different initial guesses in gradient descent is also covered, showing how they can yield different optimal estimates.

children.title: Cross Entropy Loss vs. Squared Loss in Classification
children.summary: Cross entropy loss is favored for classification tasks due to its ability to heavily penalize incorrect predictions, particularly at extremes of zero and one, while squared loss has drawbacks such as being non-convex and bounded, which can lead to local minima and less effective error measurement. The segment covers model selection and evaluation steps, including choosing models like linear or logistic regression, selecting appropriate loss functions, fitting models with regularization, and evaluating performance using metrics like R-squared. It also details the definition of cross-entropy loss for binary labels, explaining its behavior as predicted probabilities change, with penalties becoming infinitely large as probabilities approach the extremes.

children.title: Cross Entropy Loss and Squared Loss Pitfalls
children.summary: Cross entropy loss is preferred for classification tasks as it penalizes incorrect predictions more heavily, especially at extremes of zero and one. In contrast, squared loss has pitfalls: it is non-convex, which can lead to local minima, and it is bounded, making it less effective for measuring model error. The segment discusses how different initial guesses in gradient descent can yield different optimal estimates. It also outlines the steps for model selection and evaluation, including choosing a model (like linear or logistic regression), selecting a loss function (squared, absolute, or cross-entropy loss), fitting the model with regularization, and evaluating it using metrics such as R-squared.

children.title: Loss Functions in Classification Tasks
children.summary: The segment discusses the loss function used in logistic regression, emphasizing its relevance in modern AI applications for classification tasks. It outlines the steps involved in model selection, including choosing a model (like linear or logistic regression), selecting a loss function (such as squared loss, absolute loss, or cross-entropy loss), fitting the model (using regularization and tools like Sklearn or gradient descent), and evaluating model performance. The cross-entropy loss is defined for binary labels, detailing its behavior for predicted probabilities when the true label is either 1 or 0.

children.title: Cross-Entropy Loss Definition and Properties
children.summary: Cross-entropy loss is defined for a binary label y, where y can be either 0 or 1, and p represents the probability of y being 1. For the case when y equals 1, the loss is calculated as -log(p), indicating that as p approaches 0, the penalty becomes infinitely large. Conversely, when y equals 0, the loss is -log(1-p), meaning that if p approaches 1, the penalty also becomes infinitely large. The discussion includes graphical representations of these loss functions, highlighting their behavior as p varies.

children.title: Cross-Entropy Loss Function Explained
children.summary: The cross-entropy loss function is defined for binary labels {0, 1} and the probability of the label being 1. When y equals 1, the loss is calculated as -log(p), and when y equals 0, the loss is -log(1-p). This function can be expressed in a compact form as a product of the label and the log probabilities, highlighting its dual nature based on the class label. The explanation includes the significance of using 1 and 0 as class indicators and how the loss function simplifies depending on the value of y.

So y equals 1 is class 1, or the winning class. And it's a 0 is the losing class. I'm modeling the probability that y equals 1. And so this loss function here can be rewritten as this. So I ignore this term and I just have this term. and then y equals zero. Like was here I ignore this term. And I select this term. I get this chart down here. children.title: Defining Cross-Entropy Loss and Empirical Risk
children.summary: The segment discusses the cross-entropy loss function used in logistic regression, detailing its formulation for binary outcomes (y = 1 and y = 0). It explains how to express the empirical risk as the average of the loss across the dataset, emphasizing the optimization problem of minimizing this loss function. The convex nature of the cross-entropy curve for a single data point is noted, highlighting the presence of a global minimum.

children.title: Cross-Entropy Loss: Optimization and Analysis
children.summary: The segment covers cross-entropy loss as a key optimization function in various software packages, noting its convex nature and global minimum, which aids in optimization. It defines the average cross-entropy loss for logistic regression over a sample size n, leading to the minimization of empirical risk. A visual comparison of the convexity of cross-entropy loss versus squared loss is provided. Additionally, it contrasts cross-entropy loss with squared loss, emphasizing the greater penalties for incorrect predictions, particularly when the true label is one. A plot illustrates the characteristics of cross-entropy loss, and the negative log loss is introduced as l = -log(p), highlighting its effect on predictions. The discussion includes examples showing how penalties increase for lower predicted probabilities, with a prediction of zero resulting in infinite loss.

children.title: Cross-Entropy Loss and Optimization
children.summary: The segment discusses the importance of understanding cross-entropy loss, which is the loss function being optimized in various software packages. It describes the convex nature of the cross-entropy curve, highlighting that it has a global minimum, which facilitates optimization. The average cross-entropy loss, or empirical risk, is defined for logistic regression over a sample size n, leading to the optimization problem of finding the estimate that minimizes this risk. The segment also includes a visual representation of the convexity of the cross-entropy loss surface compared to a squared loss surface.
